text: The surgeon performs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, taking care not to breach the medial rectal fascia. The dissection is challenging due to the dominant fatty layer in the obese patient. The video includes a diagram illustrating the excision line for a mid to low rectal tumor, showing the entry into the rectovasical fascia, the position of the seminal vesicles, and the division of the mesorectum. The current step demonstrates the opening of the rectovasical fascia.

The surgeon identifies the superior hypogastric plexus at the sacrum level and dissects the right hypogastric nerve from the mesorectal fascial envelope. This nerve-preserving technique reveals the holy plane, a bloodless space between the mesorectal fascia and the endopelvic fascia.
The surgeon opens the mesorectal fascial plane, carefully dissecting around the rectum to free it at multiple levels.

The surgeon dissects into the adenine valley of fascia, separating the prostate from the rectum, covered by the Denonvilliers fascia. The rectum is divided at the mid to low rectum level after achieving an adequate resection margin. The splenic flexure has been mobilized, and the left colon is prepared for the insertion of the CDH-XXIX gun. An endostapler is used to resect the rectum.
The surgeon checks the anastomosis underwater, ensuring there is no tension on the connection, as the operation concludes.
